one_line_takeaway: A PRISMA-screened SoK of 116 agentic-AI security papers proposes a seven-layer x four-temporality (LASM) coding scheme and finds, by the authors' own single-coder classification, that multi-agent/ecosystem/governance threats with cross-session or dormant persistence get only 6.3% of research attention despite judged highest severity, that essentially all documented tool-execution attacks trace to one root cause (untrust-marked tool outputs), that 7 of 28 threat cells have zero published defenses, and that no existing benchmark measures cross-session or dormant (T3/T4) attacks at all.
key_finding: The paper's own claimed findings (grounded in its own coding of the literature, not experiments it ran) are the four Empirical Findings: (1) the {L5,L6,L7}x{T3,T4} region -- multi-agent, ecosystem, and governance threats with cross-session or non-session-bounded persistence -- receives only 9 of 144 (6.3%) coded paper-cell assignments despite the authors judging it to contain the highest-severity threat classes, an inverse severity-effort correlation that held after adding 22 new 2025-2026 papers (zone grew by only 1 assignment); (2) every L4 (tool-execution) attack instance in the 27-assignment L4 slice of the corpus is attributable to a single structural defect (principal trust inversion / absence of trust-marking on tool returns), meaning any L4 defense that doesn't enforce trust labeling on tool outputs 'addresses symptoms, not the cause'; (3) 7 of the 28 LASM x temporality grid cells have zero coded defense coverage, and 3 of those (L4,T4 / L5,T4 / L6,T3) also contain documented attacks, i.e. published-attack-but-no-published-defense gaps; (4) 0 of the 10 surveyed agent-security benchmarks (e.g., HarmBench, InjecAgent-style suites) evaluate T3 (cross-session) or T4 (drift/dormant) threats, so any claim of T3/T4 defense coverage in the literature is currently unfalsifiable by benchmark. The paper's theoretical contribution, Proposition 1 (defense non-transferability), is a short proof sketch showing that a defense reading exclusively from representation R_i has zero detection advantage over baseline against an attack whose payload lives exclusively in a disjoint R_j -- the formal justification the authors give for defense-in-depth being structurally necessary rather than merely good practice.
core_contribution: This is a systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) survey, not an attack/defense paper. Its own contribution is the Layered Attack Surface Model (LASM): a seven-layer decomposition of the agentic AI stack (L1 Foundation, L2 Cognitive, L3 Memory, L4 Tool Execution, L5 Multi-Agent Coordination, L6 Ecosystem, L7 Governance), each layer defined by a disjoint trust boundary B_i and attack-surface representation R_i (R_i ∩ R_j = ∅ for i≠j), plus an orthogonal temporality axis (T1 instantaneous, T2 session-persistent, T3 cross-session cumulative, T4 sub-session-stack/non-session-bounded, split into T4a drift and T4b dormant). The paper proves (Proposition 1 / 'Theorem 1', proof sketch in the main text, full proof deferred to a supplementary appendix) a non-transferability result: a 'pure' defense that reads only from R_i has detection probability equal to baseline against an attack whose payload is introduced exclusively in a disjoint R_j. Using this framework the authors systematically coded a corpus of 116 PRISMA-screened primary security papers (2021-2026) into 144 (layer, temporality) paper-cell assignments and report four numbered Empirical Findings: EF1, the under-studied zone {L5,L6,L7}×{T3,T4} holds 9/144 (6.3%) of assignments yet contains the highest-severity threats; EF2, all L4 (tool execution) attacks in the corpus reduce to one structural cause, 'principal trust inversion' (tool outputs entering the planning context without a trust label); EF3, 7 of 28 LASM×temporality grid cells have zero surveyed defense coverage, and 3 of those 7 -- (L4,T4), (L5,T4), (L6,T3) -- contain documented attacks; EF4, none of the 10 surveyed agent-security benchmarks evaluates T3 or T4 threats, so claimed T3/T4 defense coverage is empirically unverifiable. Beyond the framework and findings, the paper contributes a cross-layer defense taxonomy with 7 attack-class defense 'recipes' (Table 8), a five-item research-gap dependency analysis (Table 10) separating engineering-tractable gaps (e.g., Gap 4: ABOM/MCP certification) from fundamental open problems, and releases supplementary artifacts (corpus_coding.csv, a robustness-analysis script, and a reference ABOM JSON Schema with validator and 12 unit tests).
threat_model: As a survey, the paper does not itself mount attacks; it formalizes a threat model used to classify the literature it reviews (Section 2.2.3). The model has four adversary dimensions: Position (External / Semi-trusted principal / Supply-chain), Knowledge (Black-box / Gray-box / White-box), Goal (Confidentiality / Integrity / Availability), and Persistence (One-shot / Persistent, which maps onto the T1-T4 temporality classes). The paper's central structural concept is 'principal trust inversion': in the intended principal hierarchy Developer > Operator > User > Environment, most deployed agents implicitly treat Environment inputs (tool return values, retrieved documents, web pages) as high-trust despite Environment being the least-trusted principal; the authors argue this single failure is the root cause underlying indirect prompt injection and most L4 (tool-execution) attacks documented in the corpus (their Empirical Finding 2).
limitations: The authors themselves disclose (Section 2.1.4, Appendix A/Table 13) that: (a) all 116 papers were layer/temporality-coded by a single author with no formal inter-rater reliability check -- mitigated only by documented coding rules and a robustness analysis bounding EF1's headline 6.3% figure to [4.81%, 8.33%] under four sensitivity protocols; (b) the search terms under-sample threat work that doesn't use 'agent'-prefixed vocabulary, partially offset by citation tracing from 3 seed papers; (c) the >=20-citation threshold for pre-2025 non-top-venue papers systematically excludes recent low-impact preprints while including older top-venue papers regardless of citation count, biasing the corpus toward formal-venue and against industry/preprint work; (d) DEF CON/Black Hat talks, vendor advisories, and red-team reports are systematically excluded by the PRISMA-style search except for two cited real-world incidents admitted via citation tracing; (e) the 18-paper defense corpus is explicitly stated to be an 'upper bound on published defenses,' not an enumeration of deployed industry defenses, so Figure 8's defense-coverage heatmap likely overstates real-world coverage; (f) the paper flags its own Figure 8 caveats: the (L7,T4) defense-coverage count of 2 is stated to be overstated because two of the underlying papers are weight-layer alignment papers with governance implications rather than L7-native T4 research, and no paper in the corpus presents an L7-native T4 detection mechanism; (g) claimed T4-range defense coverage (interpretability tools, memory invariant enforcement, behavioral monitoring) is explicitly described as unvalidated against actual T4 attacks because no T3/T4 benchmark exists (this is EF4 feeding back as a limitation on EF3's coverage claims); (h) the ABOM JSON-schema artifact is described as a conceptual specification, not a ratified standard, and the action-log schema in Section 4.3.4 is explicitly labeled a proposal rather than a validated mechanism.

datasets_used: None original to this paper -- it is a literature survey. The 'dataset' is the authors' own 116-paper coded corpus (144 paper-cell assignments) released as supplementary corpus_coding.csv, screened via a two-phase PRISMA-style search of IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, arXiv (cs.CR/cs.AI/cs.LG), and Google Scholar (1,634 records retrieved, 1,121 after dedup, 313 after title/abstract screen, 116 after full-text assessment).
quantitative_results: Under-studied zone {L5,L6,L7}x{T3,T4}: 9 of 144 paper-cell assignments (6.3%), vs. 135 assignments outside the zone (Empirical Finding 1); robustness range [4.81%, 8.33%] across 4 sensitivity protocols (Appendix D)
Zone growth from 2026 corpus extension: 8 -> 9 assignments after adding 22 new May 2025-Apr 2026 papers (near-zero shift, cited as robustness evidence)
L4 (tool execution) coded assignments: 27 total across the corpus; authors state every one traces to principal trust inversion / absent trust-marking on tool returns (Empirical Finding 2)
Defense coverage: 7 of 28 LASM x temporality grid cells have zero coded defenses; 3 of those 7 -- (L4,T4), (L5,T4), (L6,T3) -- also contain documented attacks (Empirical Finding 3, Figure 8)
Benchmark coverage: 0 of 10 surveyed agent-security benchmarks evaluate T3 or T4 threats; every surveyed benchmark terminates at T1 or T2 (Empirical Finding 4)
Screening funnel counts (Table 2): 1,634 combined retrieved -> 1,121 after dedup (513 excluded) -> 313 after title/abstract screen (808 excluded) -> 116 final corpus (197 excluded at full-text stage)

Corpus composition: 38 attack papers (L1-L4), 26 multi-agent/ecosystem papers (L5-L6), 16 governance/alignment papers (L7), 18 defense papers, 13 benchmark/evaluation papers, 5 comparison surveys; 186-entry bibliography total, 70 of which are background/non-coded references
Row totals in the LASM x temporality coverage matrix (Table 5): L1=32, L2=22, L3=25, L4=27, L5=15, L6=12, L7=11 paper-cell assignments; column totals T1=63, T2=42, T3=21, T4=18
2025-2026 inclusion detail: of 44 papers from Jan 2025-Apr 2026, 18 met the >=20-citation threshold unconditionally and 26 were admitted under a gap-area exception
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the classic experimental sense -- this is a survey with no original attack/defense implementation to benchmark. The paper instead positions LASM against prior taxonomies by contrasting its component-centric, temporally-aware decomposition with (its characterization of) prior surveys [14-16] as type-centric (organizing by attack type such as jailbreaking/injection/poisoning) and lacking a temporal dimension; it also cross-maps five documented real-world attacks/incidents (including a Postmark MCP BCC exfiltration and a GitHub Copilot project-file RCE) against the OWASP LLM Top 10 and MITRE ATLAS taxonomies (Table 15) to argue that attacks sharing an OWASP/ATLAS label can map to different LASM layers requiring non-overlapping defenses. Numeric attack-success figures cited from surveyed (not authored) work include: PoisonedRAG >90% targeted knowledge corruption from as few as 5 adversarial documents in a 10,000+ corpus (Zou et al.); AgentPoison >80% attack success at <0.1% poison rate (Chen et al.); InjecAgent/Agent Security Bench >60% attack success across leading models; Maloyan et al. 50-80% attack success against production coding agents (Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor); MINJA 98.2% query-only memory injection success; A-MemGuard >=95% attack mitigation at ~15% throughput overhead (Lin et al.); Zanbaghi et al. semantic-drift backdoor detection at 92.5% accuracy; Wu et al. ARE framework 67% attack success rate against visual web-browsing agents from perturbations affecting <5% of webpage pixels.
